claim: The encoding drops pharyngeal consonants (Syriac het and ayin have no EVA representation) — consistent with a non-Semitic (European) copyist who lacks these phonemes.
evidence_for: Lexicon 'yn'=aynā ('eye', 699 tokens) matches without the initial ayin
Pharmaceutical vocabulary with het (kuhla, mashha, hamra) initially invisible without this assumption
evidence_against: Dropping phonemes creates systematic homograph ambiguity (e.g., 'kl' = kol/kuhla/kalla)
Convenient assumption that rescues otherwise unmatched vocabulary
test: Test whether reintroducing het/ayin assumptions changes coverage significantly; count homograph collisions produced
test_threshold: Structural argument more than numerical test. Evaluate qualitatively.

tests_run.details: Zipfian distribution (0.8946) confirms natural language patterns, but pharyngeal consonant dropping requires plaintext knowledge to test directly; high hapax ratio (0.701) suggests complex encoding, making the hypothesis plausible but statistically inconclusive.

reasoning: Linguistic plausibility claim; numerical test secondary.
source: Brady Defibaugh (April 2026), A Syriac Pharmaceutical Dispensatory Encoded in the Voynich Manuscript (DANI pipeline v31)
